General Information.PAD Prevalence: It has been estimated that at least 113 million people worldwide have PAD.
General Information.Guideline Focus: The document provides a contemporary guideline for the diagnosis and management of patients with lower extremity PAD.

terms.ALI: Acute (≤ 2-wk) hypoperfusion of the limb that may be characterized by pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia, and/or paralysis.
terms.Anatomic level: Anatomic subsets to localize disease in the lower extremity in PAD patients.
terms.Angiosome-based blood flow: Uninterrupted arterial flow to anatomical territory of a skin region, usually with a wound, supplied by a specific angiosome artery.
terms.Claudication: Fatigue, cramping, aching, pain, or other discomfort in the muscles of the lower extremities during exercise and relieved by rest.
terms.CLTI: Chronic limb-threatening ischemia, characterized by rest pain, nonhealing wounds, or gangrene.
terms.Endovascular revascularization: Catheter-based procedures like angioplasty and stenting.
terms.Functional status: Patient's ability to meet basic needs, fulfill usual roles, and maintain health and well-being.
terms.Hybrid revascularization: Approach that includes endovascular and surgical components.
terms.In-line (pulsatile) blood flow: Uninterrupted arterial flow via the main infra-popliteal arteries to the foot.
terms.Inflow vs outflow: Distinctions between proximal and distal arterial flows.
terms.MACE: Major adverse cardiovascular events such as death, myocardial infarction, or stroke.
terms.MALE: Major adverse limb events, usually requiring amputation or surgical intervention.
terms.Multispecialty care team for PAD: A team of professionals for evaluation and management of PAD.
terms.Regions of the foot: Defines anatomical segments of the foot.
terms.Structured community-based exercise prog: Self-directed with guidance from health care professionals.
terms.Structured exercise program: Qualified health care professional recommendations for exercise.
terms.Supervised exercise therapy: Exercise programs in hospital or outpatient facilities.
terms.Surgical revascularization: Surgical procedures to remove plaque, thromboectomy or bypass to restore blood flow.
terms.Thrombolysis: Administration of thrombolytic agents to reconstruct arterial blood flow.
terms.Tissue loss: Minor - Nonhealing ulcer; Major - Loss of tissue beyond transmetatarsal level.
terms.WIfI: Clinical staging system for patients with CLTI based on the wound, ischemia, and foot infection.

Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 1 (Strong): Benefit >>> Risk. Recommendations should be performed or administered.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 2a (Moderate): Benefit >> Risk. Recommendations are reasonable and can be useful/effective.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 2b (Weak): Benefit ≥ Risk. Recommendations may/might be reasonable.

Class 3 (No Benefit or Harm).No Benefit: Benefit = Risk. Not recommended as it is not indicated/useful/effective.
Class 3 (No Benefit or Harm).Harm: Risk > Benefit. Recommendations are potentially harmful and should not be performed.

Level of Evidence (LOE).Level A: High-quality evidence from more than one RCT or meta-analyses.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level B-R: Moderate-quality evidence from one or more RCTs.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-LD: Evidence from limited data or studies with design limitations.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-EO: Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience.

content.clinical_assessment_for_PAD: Clinical assessment is a central component of evaluation for PAD. Recognition of clinical subsets of PAD guides clinical evaluation, diagnostic testing, and treatment, and determines the urgency of care. The assessment includes history, physical examination, and consideration of differential diagnoses.
content.clinical_subsets_of_PAD: Patients with confirmed PAD are categorized into four subsets: asymptomatic PAD, chronic symptomatic PAD, chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI), and acute limb ischemia (ALI). Patients may develop different symptoms over time and move between these subsets.

figure.title: Clinical Subsets of PAD
figure.description: Diagram showing progression and relationship between different subsets of PAD: Asymptomatic, Chronic Symptomatic, CLTI, and ALI.

Asymptomatic PAD.Prevalence: 20%-59% of patients with objectively proven PAD report no leg symptoms.
Characteristics: Functional impairment may be comparable to symptomatic PAD patients.
Asymptomatic PAD.Risks: Increased risk of MACE, including mortality.

Symptoms: Includes progressive exercise intensity and relieved by rest.
Characteristics: Pain in buttocks, thigh, calf, or foot.
Not improving during walking; relieved within 10 minutes of rest.
May include tightness, numbness, burning, or shooting.

CLTI.Prevalence: Incidence estimated to be between 1% and 20%.
CLTI.Symptoms: Ischemic rest pain, nonhealing wounds/ulcers, gangrene.
CLTI.Risk Factors: Higher rates of amputation, ischemia often worsened with limb elevation and dependency.

ALI.Characteristics: Sudden decrease in arterial perfusion of the leg.
Symptoms: Acute (<2 weeks) pain, pallor, coolness, paresthesias, potential for paralysis.
ALI.Causes: Embolus, thrombosis, trauma, or peripheral aneurysm.
ALI.Risk Factors: Lower ABI values associated with increased risk.

content.title: History and Physical Examination to Assess for PAD
content.synopsis: Clinical assessment for PAD begins with recognizing risk factors for PAD to assist in identifying patients at risk, including assessing lower extremity symptoms and checking for abnormal physical signs such as pulse deficits. Patients at increased risk include those with atherosclerotic conditions and specific demographic factors. Diagnostic evaluation may involve the ankle-brachial index (ABI).

recommendations.text: In patients at increased risk of PAD, perform a comprehensive medical history and review of symptoms to assess for exertional leg symptoms, lower extremity rest pain, and wounds or ischemic skin changes.
In patients at increased risk of PAD, perform a comprehensive vascular examination and inspection of the legs and feet.

patients_at_increased_risk: Age 50-64 years with risk factors for atherosclerosis or family history of PAD
Age <50 years with diabetes and additional risk factors for atherosclerosis
Individuals with known atherosclerotic disease in other vascular areas
history_findings: History of nonhealing or slow-healing lower extremity wound

Conditions.Other Characteristics: Symptoms variable; history of degenerative arthritis

Conditions.Effect of Exercise: After variable degree of exercise; may also be present at rest
Conditions.Effect of Position: May be relieved by not bearing weight

Conditions.Effect of Exercise: Induced by sitting, standing, or walking (variable)
Conditions.Other Characteristics: History of back problems; worse with sitting; relief when supine or standing

Conditions.Condition: Spinal stenosis (degenerative disc disease or tumor)
Conditions.Effect of Rest: Variable relief but can take a long time to recover
Conditions.Other Characteristics: Worse with standing and extending spine
History of iliofemoral deep vein thrombosis; edema; signs of venous stasis

content.Diagnostic Evaluation: Patients at increased risk of PAD require thorough vascular examination focusing on lower extremities. Proper care in pulse assessment and additional evaluation for peripheral neuropathy should be considered.

content.Title: Diagnostic Testing for PAD
content.Purpose: Guidelines for diagnostic testing in suspected PAD, focusing on the combination of history, physical examination, ABI, and other tests.

Key Points.1: History and physical exams are crucial to identify signs of PAD.
Key Points.2: ABI is recommended with ankle pulse volume recordings or Doppler waveforms to diagnose PAD.
Key Points.3: Abnormal ABI is categorized as abnormal (≤0.90), borderline (0.91-0.99), normal (1.00-1.40), or noncompressible (>1.40).
Key Points.4: Screening patients with increased risk of PAD is reasonable; however, it is not recommended for those without risk and symptoms.
Key Points.5: Pressure/toe-brachial index (TBI) with waveforms should be done if ABI >1.40.
Key Points.6: Exercise treadmill ABI testing is recommended for suspected chronic PAD symptoms.
Key Points.7: Exercise treadmill ABI can assess walking performance for patients with PAD and abnormal ABI.
Key Points.8: Reasonable to perform segmental leg pressures and Doppler waveforms for chronic PAD with symptoms.
Key Points.9: Toe pressure/TBI with waveforms, skin perfusion pressure, and others are recommended for suspected CLTI.
Key Points.10: For CLTI with nonhealing wounds, use toe pressure/TBI and other measures to assess healing likelihood.

content.Conclusion: The ABI along with other tests helps in diagnosing PAD and guiding treatment, especially in asymptomatic, chronic, or CLTI cases.

content.Algorithm: Diagnostic Testing for Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD)

Sections.Title: Patients with suspected or at risk of PAD (excluding CLTI)
Tests: Resting ABI with or without PVR and/or Doppler waveforms.

Decisions.Action: Exercise treadmill ABI or TBI with waveforms

Sections.Title: Patients with suspected CLTI
Tests: TBI with waveforms, TcPO₂, and/or SPP

Decisions.Action: TBI with waveforms, TcPO₂, and/or SPP (2a)

Next Steps.Action: Chronic symptomatic PAD or asymptomatic PAD management

content.Measurement: The resting ABI is measured using a blood pressure cuff and a Doppler device to detect blood flow and pressure in the pedal and brachial arteries.
content.ABI Ratio: The ABI ratio is the higher systolic pressure in the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial arteries divided by the higher of the left and right brachial artery systolic pressures.
content.Sensitivity and Specificity: For PAD symptoms, the resting ABI has a sensitivity of 69% to 79% and a specificity of 83% to 99%, compared with imaging studies.
content.Noncompressible Arteries: Occur in conditions like diabetes or CKD, potentially leading to falsely elevated ABI.
content.Screening and Risk: Screening for PAD using ABI among those under 50 has low prevalence, but higher in older populations or those with risk factors.

Development: Develops in regions of skin changes due to chronic venous disease and local venous hypertension
Typical Characteristics: Wound drainage rather than dry lesion

content.ABI Measurement: The exercise treadmill ABI is measured within 1 and 5 minutes after exercise on a motorized treadmill using a Doppler device and a blood pressure cuff. ABI measurements may be repeated until recovery to baseline.
Exercise Testing: Shows highest sensitivity and specificity for PAD diagnosis in patients with resting ABI <= 0.90
content.Importance of ABI: Used to evaluate functional status and severity in symptomatic PAD patients.

content.revascularization: Standardized nomenclature for Doppler waveforms from lower extremity arteries has recently been proposed.
content.ABI assessment: The ABI alone may be inadequate to assess patients with suspected CLTI. Concordance between ABI and toe pressure/TBI among patients with CLTI is poor.
content.TBI: A TBI of ≤0.70 is considered abnormal and allows for diagnosis of PAD.
content.toe pressures: Pressures <30 mm Hg are reflective of severe ischemia.
content.perfusion assessment: TcPO2 and SPP are other testing modalities that can be used.
content.Doppler flowmetry: Emerging technology with potential for assessing perfusion in limbs with wounds.
content.pacing acceleration time: Correlated with the ABI and clinical presentation of PAD.
content.imaging recommendations: For patients with functional limitation or CLTI, duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA, or catheter angiography are recommended.

content.Synopsis: Arterial imaging studies are used primarily when revascularization is being considered or when PAD diagnosis is inconclusive. Imaging can be done through various noninvasive methods like duplex ultrasound, CTA, and MRA. These modalities offer different benefits and risks, such as avoiding radiation but potentially requiring contrast which may not be suitable for all patients.
Imaging Modalities: Duplex ultrasound: Noninvasive, no radiation or contrast required.
CTA and MRA: Noninvasive cross-sectional imaging; CTA requires contrast and involves radiation risks.
Key Points: Preprocedure imaging assesses vascular status and revascularization feasibility.
Choice of imaging is patient-specific based on modality and resource availability.
Noninvasive imaging is crucial for planning in CLTI and PAD cases with unclear diagnosis.
CTA and catheter angiography can pose risks due to radiation and contrast.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses the assessment and management of patients with Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD), emphasizing the importance of identifying risk amplifiers like diabetes, CKD, depression, and polyvascular disease. Revascularization procedures are considered when clinically indicated. The guideline also stresses understanding health disparities and social determinants of health that impact PAD outcomes.
content.Special Considerations: It highlights the significance of evaluating risk factors that amplify major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and major adverse limb events (MALE) in PAD patients, stressing the combined effect of conditions such as polyvascular disease and diabetes. Depression is also noted as a prevalent issue that affects PAD prognosis.
content.Recommendations: Clinicians should assess for PAD-related risk amplifiers when developing treatment recommendations, acknowledging demographic factors and health disparities.

content.Title: Health Disparities and PAD-Related Risk Amplifiers Increase Risk of MACE and MALE
Contributors to Health Disparities: Race and Ethnicity (especially Black, Hispanic, American Indian individuals)
Geography (e.g., rural location with less access to health care)
PAD-Related Risk Amplifiers: Older Age (e.g., >75 years) and Geriatric Syndromes (e.g., frailty, mobility impairment)
Ongoing Smoking and Other Tobacco Use
Chronic Kidney Disease and End-Stage Kidney Disease
Polyvascular Disease (i.e., coexisting atherosclerotic heart-brain-leg cardiovascular disease)
Social Determinants of Health: Lower Quality Education and Poor Health Literacy
Lower Income and Less Access to Quality Housing
Limited Access to Quality Food and Exercise
Poor Access to Health Care (preventive care, diagnosis, treatment, revascularization)
content.Clinical Impact: Recognition of the clinical impact of these amplifiers is crucial in developing a treatment plan. Longitudinal follow-up and education regarding risk of progression to CLTI are important.
Associated Conditions: Depressive symptoms associated with higher mortality rates (MACE and MALE) in patients with PAD

Risk Factors.Older Age: Age 75 and older is a significant risk factor for PAD.
Risk Factors.Diabetes: Major risk factor with significant increase in all-cause death and major adverse cardiovascular events.
Risk Factors.Smoking: Strong association with increased risk of PAD-related hospitalizations.
Risk Factors.CKD: Chronic Kidney Disease significantly raises the risk for cardiovascular events and mortality.
Risk Factors.ESKD: Higher risk of mortality and events compared with non-ESKD population.
Risk Factors.Polyvascular Disease: Increases vascular event risk and complications.
Risk Factors.Microvascular Disease: Linked with higher risk of PAD events.
Risk Factors.Depression: Associated with increased mortality in PAD patients.

Outcomes and Associations.MACE: Major adverse cardiovascular events, strongly linked with diabetes and CKD.
Outcomes and Associations.Revascularization: Outcomes vary based on CKD status and diabetes presence.
Outcomes and Associations.Mortality: Varies widely based on the presence of co-morbid conditions like CKD.

content.Recommendation: Clinicians and health care systems should actively pursue evidence of health disparities in diagnosis, treatment, and outcomes for patients with Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD), and use efforts to limit the impact of these disparities on clinical outcomes.
content.Synopsis: This section addresses disparities in PAD detection, management, and outcomes in the U.S. High-risk racial and ethnic groups, along with patients of lower socioeconomic status and those in rural areas, face disproportionately higher rates of Major Adverse Limb Events (MALE). These disparities are rooted in social determinants like housing, health care access, and economic inequities, which have been linked to chronic stress and adverse health outcomes.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Evidence shows health disparities are common for patients with PAD, making it difficult to discern distinct health patterns. Addressing disparities involves identifying at-risk populations and implementing comprehensive care plans. Efforts also include post-diagnosis management strategies like guideline-directed medical therapy, recognizing the impact of socioeconomic factors, and ensuring thorough follow-up for patients, especially those from disadvantaged backgrounds.

content.AHA initiatives: AHA emphasizes reducing lower extremity amputations, promoting quality measures for PAD care, and addressing health disparities.
content.PAD Prevalence: Higher among Black patients than White, with disparities noted.
content.Race and Ethnicity: Different rates and outcomes among Black, American Indian, and Hispanic patients.
content.Amputation Risks: Higher among minority groups, with barriers in access to care contributing.
content.Race Impact: Disparities in PAD outcomes are linked to racial and ethnic backgrounds.
content.Health Disparities: Emphasis on addressing socioeconomic and racial factors affecting PAD care.

content.Clinical Information: Peripheral artery disease (PAD) disproportionately affects older adults, particularly those over 75 years and even more so over 80 years. PAD identification in older patients involves thorough assessment, including physical examinations of the legs and feet. The presence of geriatric syndromes like frailty, sarcopenia, malnutrition, and mobility impairment increases risks such as MACE (major adverse cardiovascular events), MALE (major adverse limb events), and death. In older adults, these conditions could worsen PAD outcomes, including the necessity for revascularization or amputation. The geographic location of patients (e.g., rural vs. urban) also influences the prevalence and outcomes of PAD due to social determinants of health.

Frailty.Assessment: Using measures such as Clinical Frailty Scale, modified Frailty Index, Risk Analysis Index.
Frailty.Association: Elevated rates of MACE associated with frailty and claudication.
Frailty.Survival: Frailty reduces survival rate; high predictor of 30-d mortality rate for all PAD revascularization procedures.

Sarcopenia.Prevalence: 10 times more prevalent in those with PAD than age-matched controls.
Sarcopenia.Association: Lower survival rate and higher risk of MACE and MALE.
Sarcopenia.Risk: Increased risk for muscle mass loss in lower extremities.

Malnutrition.Prevalence: Common in older patients with PAD, affecting up to 50%.
Malnutrition.Survival: 5-y survival rate directly related to GNRI stratification.
Malnutrition.Mortality: 3.0-d mortality 5 times higher in severe malnutrition compared to moderate/mild.

Mobility Impairment.Association: Poor physical function in PAD vs. non-PAD.
Mobility Impairment.Ambulatory Patients: Patients >75 y/o with PAD 13.5:1-fold more limited in pain.
Mobility Impairment.Mortality: Patients >65 y/o with PAD had a faster decline in mobility and higher mortality.

Revascularization Considerations.Age Factor: Age >80 associated with increased mortality rate post-revascularization.
Revascularization Considerations.Surgery: Patients >70 with CLTI dependent on functional status.
Revascularization Considerations.Treatment: Older less likely to be prescribed GDMT post-revascularization.

Impact of Amputation.Mortality: Higher mortality associated with amputation in older patients.
Impact of Amputation.Risk Increase: Mortality rates increased 4% yearly over age 85.
Impact of Amputation.Outcome Post-CLTI: Higher risk for surgery correlated with lower 3.0-d mortality.

Polypharmacy.Management: Tailoring of medical therapies and shared decision-making to minimize impact.

Medical Therapy and Preventive Foot Care.Purpose: To reduce risk of progression and limb-threatening conditions and improve leg symptoms.
Medical Therapy and Preventive Foot Care.Preventive Care: Implemented to prevent ulceration and risk of CLTI.

content.Title: Antiplatelet and Antithrombotic Therapy for PAD
content.Description: Recommendations for antiplatelet and antithrombotic therapy to reduce the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and major adverse limb events (MALE) in patients with peripheral artery disease (PAD).

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with symptomatic PAD, single antiplatelet therapy is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with symptomatic PAD, single antiplatelet therapy with clopidogrel alone (75 mg daily) is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with symptomatic PAD, single antiplatelet therapy with aspirin alone (range, 75-325 mg daily) is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with symptomatic PAD, low-dose rivaroxaban (2.5 mg twice daily) combined with low-dose aspirin is effective to reduce the risk of MACE and MALE.
After endovascular or surgical revascularization for PAD, antiplatelet therapy is recommended.
After endovascular or surgical revascularization for PAD, low-dose rivaroxaban (2.5 mg twice daily) combined with low-dose aspirin is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE and MALE.
After endovascular revascularization for PAD, dual antiplatelet therapy with a P2Y12 antagonist and low-dose aspirin is reasonable for at least 1 to 6 months.

After endovascular or surgical revascularization in patients with PAD who require full-intensity anticoagulation for another indication and are not at high risk of bleeding, adding single antiplatelet therapy is reasonable.
In patients with asymptomatic PAD, single antiplatelet therapy is reasonable to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with symptomatic PAD without recent revascularization, the benefit of dual antiplatelet therapy is uncertain.
In patients with symptomatic PAD, the benefit of vorapaxar added to existing antiplatelet therapy is uncertain.
After surgical revascularization for PAD with a prosthetic graft, dual antiplatelet therapy with a P2Y12 antagonist and low-dose aspirin may be reasonable for at least 1 month.
In patients with PAD without another indication (e.g., atrial fibrillation), full-intensity oral anticoagulation should not be used to reduce the risk of MACE and MALE.

content.Synopsis: Antiplatelet and antithrombotic therapies are key components of medical therapy for patients with PAD. Aspirin or other antiplatelet drugs reduce PAD, CAD, and cerebrovascular disease risk. Dual antiplatelet therapy use is uncertain due to bleeding risk. Rivaroxaban with low-dose aspirin reduces ischemic events more effectively than aspirin alone for those at high ischemic risk. Anticoagulation without indication is harmful.
Recommendations: Single antiplatelet therapy reduces major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) in symptomatic PAD patients.
The antiplatelet agents evaluated include clopidogrel and ticagrelor, showing various efficacy and safety profiles.
The COMPASS trial analyzed low-dose rivaroxaban combined with aspirin, reducing MACE and major adverse limb events (MALE).
Post-revascularization antiplatelet therapy is common but inadequately studied, with paclitaxel-coated devices under investigation for efficacy and safety.

Asymptomatic PAD Management: Cardiovascular risk reduction with lipid-lowering, antihypertensive therapy, and diabetes management.
Single antiplatelet therapy (SAPT) with a class rating of 2a.

No Recent Revascularization: Single antiplatelet therapy (SAPT): Clopidogrel 75 mg daily or Aspirin 75-325 mg daily.
Low-dose aspirin plus rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID.
Recent Revascularization: Low-dose aspirin plus rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID. Indicated after endovascular revascularization.
Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): Low-dose aspirin plus P2Y12 inhibitor for surgical revascularization with a prosthetic graft.
Single antiplatelet therapy with full-intensity anticoagulation for cases with atrial fibrillation, VTE, or other indications.

Additional Management Recommendations: Structured exercise for chronic symptomatic PAD.
Preventive and management strategies for chronic symptomatic PAD, including pressure offloading and CLTI treatment.
Clinical Trials and Studies: VOYAGER PAD trial showed benefits of low-dose aspirin and low-dose rivaroxaban for improving MACE and MALE outcomes.
Dual antiplatelet therapy is effective post-revascularization.
Triple therapy increases bleeding risk but is justified for certain clinical indications.

content.therapy: Antiplatelet therapy for PAD patients undergoing revascularization if they are not at high bleeding risk. ACC guidelines focus on anticoagulation for coronary interventions.
content.asymptomatic PAD: Patients with asymptomatic PAD are at increased risk of MACE. Clinical trials like CLIPS examined aspirin's effect but were underpowered.
content.Vorapaxar: An antiplatelet agent shown to lower MALE risk but can increase bleeding risk. It's contraindicated in patients with a stroke history.

content.guideline_summary: The guideline is focused on lipid-lowering therapy for Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD). It emphasizes the use of high-intensity statin therapy to achieve at least a 50% reduction in LDL-C levels. For patients with PAD who are already on maximally tolerated statin therapy and still have elevated LDL-C, adding PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe is recommended. The guideline also discusses the benefit of lipid-lowering in reducing major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and major adverse limb events (MALE).

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with PAD, treatment with high-intensity statin therapy is indicated, with an aim of achieving a ≥50% reduction in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) level.
In patients with PAD who are on maximally tolerated statin therapy and have an LDL-C level of ≥70 mg/dL, it is reasonable to add PCSK9 inhibitor therapy.
In patients with PAD who are on maximally tolerated statin therapy and have an LDL-C level of ≥70 mg/dL, it is reasonable to add ezetimibe therapy.

content.clinical_context: Dyslipidemia is a common risk factor in PAD patients, and managing LDL-C levels through statin and additional therapies improves outcomes. The guideline references evidence from RCTs and observational studies supporting these interventions.

genes.context: In patients with PAD who are on maximally tolerated statin therapy and have an LDL-C level of ≥70 mg/dL, it is reasonable to add PCSK9 inhibitor therapy. In a subgroup analysis of studies evaluating PCSK9 inhibitors, use of alirocumab and evolocumab in patients with PAD were associated with lower rates of MACE and MALE compared with placebo.

Antihypertensive Therapy for PAD.Recommendation: In patients with PAD and hypertension, antihypertensive therapy should be administered to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with PAD and hypertension, a systolic blood pressure (SBP) goal of <130 mm Hg and a diastolic blood pressure target of <80 mm Hg is recommended.
In patients with PAD and hypertension, the selective use of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors or angiotensin-receptor blockers is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE.

genes.context: This recommendation for the use of PCSK9 inhibitors in patients with PAD who have not achieved an LDL target of <70 mg/dL aligns with the 2018 AHA/ACC cholesterol guideline recommendation for patients with clinical atherosclerotic CVD.

content.Synopsis: Hypertension is the most common cardiovascular risk factor found in 35% to 55% of patients diagnosed with PAD. Hypertension is associated with a longitudinal decline in ABI in adults over 65 years.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Treatment of hypertension is indicated among patients with PAD to reduce the risk of MACE, including stroke, MI, heart failure, and cardiovascular death.
Lower blood pressure targets may compromise blood flow to a lower extremity with impaired perfusion caused by PAD and worsen limb symptoms.
2017 ACC/AHA hypertension clinical practice guideline recommends a goal for blood pressure of <130/80 mm Hg in patients with known cardiovascular risk.
SPRINT trial showed benefits with aggressive SBP target <120 mm Hg in patients with CVD.
HOPE trial indicated ramipril was associated with a significant reduction in risk of death, stroke, and revascularization in PAD subset.
ONTARGET trial showed similar benefits with telmisartan.
Recommendations for Smoking Cessation fo: Patients with PAD who smoke cigarettes or use any other forms of tobacco should be advised at every visit to quit or encouraged to maintain cessation.
Assistance in developing a quitting plan that includes pharmacotherapy and counseling should be provided.
Patients should avoid exposure to secondhand smoke in all settings.

content.synopsis: Cigarette smoking and other forms of tobacco use are strong, dose-responsive risk factors for PAD development. Quitting smoking is critical for reducing PAD risk, progression, and related deaths. Both pharmacological and behavioral strategies are recommended for increasing cessation rates.
recommendations: In the US, 68% of adult smokers want to quit, but only 7% succeed. Electronic nicotine delivery systems may be linked to PAD progression.
Coordinated interventions combining pharmacological and nonpharmacological methods can double or triple cessation rates.
Secondhand smoke is linked to increased risks of PAD, CVD, and possibly MALE.
notable_points: Smoking cessation is crucial due to its association with lower rates of major adverse limb events (MALE) and death in PAD patients.
Electronic nicotine delivery systems are discouraged for cessation due to insufficient data on their long-term safety.
Clinicians should actively support smoking cessation using evidence-based guidelines.

recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with PAD and type 2 diabetes, use of glucagon-like peptide-1 agonists (liraglutide and semaglutide) and sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT-2) inhibitors (canagliflozin, dapagliflozin, and empagliflozin) are effective to reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with PAD, management of diabetes should be coordinated among members of the health care team.
In patients with PAD and diabetes, glycemic control may be beneficial to improve limb outcomes.

content.summary: Diabetes is a significant risk factor for PAD and CLTI. A mix of pharmacological and nonpharmacological therapies is recommended. Updated care standards validate the use of specific drug therapies, improving outcomes in diabetic patients with PAD. A coordinated healthcare team effort is essential for effective diabetes management.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses the outcomes following ertugliflozin treatment in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus participants with vascular disease and emphasizes the significance of canagliflozin in patients with PAD. Studies indicated varying results regarding the risk of amputations, especially in patients with diabetes undergoing various trials such as CANVAS, CREDENCE, and EMPA-REG OUTCOME.
content.PAD and Diabetes: Diabetes significantly increases the risk of PAD, with data suggesting higher rates of MACE, MALE, and amputation outcomes. The EUCLID trial data emphasizes the prevalence of diabetes in PAD patients and the associated risks. Poor glycemic control is highlighted as a major risk factor for diabetic neuropathy and foot ulcers.
content.Clinical Recommendations: Management for patients with PAD includes vaccination recommendations: annual influenza and SARS-CoV-2 vaccinations, including boosters.

clinical_info: In patients at high cardiovascular risk, a diet emphasizing intake of vegetables, fruits, legumes, nuts, whole grains, and fish is beneficial for reducing risk of developing PAD and risk of MACE.
For patients with PAD, B-complex vitamin supplementation to lower homocysteine levels is not beneficial for prevention of MACE.
In patients with PAD, chelation therapy (e.g., EDTA) is not beneficial for prevention of MACE.
Vitamin D supplementation is not beneficial for prevention of MACE in patients with PAD.
Using B-complex or vitamin D or chelation therapy is not supported to reduce risk of MACE in patients with PAD.
COVID-19 vaccination is recommended for patients at high risk for complications, particularly for those with PAD.

content.Synopsis: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD and claudication, medical therapy with cilostazol has been shown to reduce symptoms of claudication and improve walking distance. In limited studies, cilostazol has also been shown to reduce restenosis after endovascular therapy for femoropopliteal stenosis. Cilostazol is contraindicated in patients with congestive heart failure. Other medical therapies, including pentoxifylline and chelation therapy, have not been shown to provide benefit in terms of leg symptoms for individuals with PAD.

Recommendations.Description: Recommended to improve leg symptoms and increase walking distance in patients with claudication.
May be useful to reduce restenosis after endovascular therapy for femoropopliteal disease.
Should not be administered in patients with PAD and congestive heart failure of any severity.
Not recommended for treatment of claudication in patients with chronic symptomatic PAD.
Not recommended for treatment of claudication in patients with chronic symptomatic PAD.

Vitamin D.Association: Lower vitamin D levels associated with increased risk of PAD.
Vitamin D.Meta-analysis: Supplementation with vitamin D showed no benefit for reduction in MACE or all-cause death.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: In a review of 15 RCTs with 3,718 participants, cilostazol improved claudication symptoms but did not affect cardiovascular deaths or QOL compared to placebo.
studies: Longitudinal prospective registry showing improved PAQ outcomes
Network meta-analysis demonstrating improvement in walking distance and ABI
Observational study showing improved QOL and limb function

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: Cilostazol's potential benefit in patients with in-stent restenosis post-femoro-popliteal stenting.
studies: Blinded RCT of cilostazol post bare-metal femoropopliteal stent treatment
Subgroup analysis of an RCT with paclitaxel-eluting stent

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: Cilostazol is a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.context: Contradicted for patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: Pentoxifylline was compared with placebo and cilostazol in claudication treatment.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.conclusion: No difference was observed in maximal walking distance between pentoxifylline and placebo.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: Chelation therapy was reviewed for its effect on claudication symptoms in PAD patients.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.conclusion: No significant improvement compared to placebo.

recommendations_for_preventive_foot_care.recommendation: Provide general preventive foot self-care education for patients with PAD and their family members and support persons.
In patients with PAD, foot inspection by a clinician at every visit is recommended.
In patients with PAD at high risk for ulcers and amputation, therapeutic footwear is recommended.
Annual comprehensive foot evaluation to identify risk factors for ulcers and amputation.
Referral to a footcare specialist for ongoing preventive care and longitudinal surveillance is reasonable.

Risk Factors: History of previous foot ulcer(s) or amputation
Chronic kidney disease (CKD), especially if end-stage
Corns or calluses on the feet
Complications: 40% rate of death at 5 years after a foot ulcer

History: Current symptoms of PAD or CLTI
Physical Examination: Evaluate pulses in the legs and feet
Other Assessments: Patient's physical limitations and self-care ability

Recommendations: Referral to a footcare specialist when needed

content.education: Self-care education is developed by the American Diabetes Association, U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.
content.proactive prevention: Ongoing foot surveillance and self-foot care education is recommended, especially for those at highest risk of ulcers.
content.foot deformities: A risk factor for foot ulcers in patients with PAD. Patients may need referral to a podiatrist or therapeutic footwear specialist.
content.foot ulcers: Assessment through comprehensive foot evaluation is recommended at least annually for patients with PAD.
content.team approach: A multispecialty care team is recommended to improve outcomes, avoid duplication of care, and ensure coordinated management.
content.exercise therapy: Structured exercise therapy is essential for patients with chronic symptomatic PAD to improve functional status.

Supervised Exercise Therapy.Description: Involves intermittent walking exercise on a treadmill, interspersed with rest periods when pain becomes moderate or severe.
Supervised Exercise Therapy.Settings: Can take place in a hospital or outpatient facility and is often placed within a cardiac rehabilitation program setting.
Supervised Exercise Therapy.Supervision: Qualified health care professionals supervise the program.
Supervised Exercise Therapy.Duration: 30-45 minutes per 60-minute session, performed at least 3 times per week for a minimum of 12 weeks.
Supervised Exercise Therapy.Goals: Progress to 30-45 minutes of active walking exercise during each session to maximum claudication pain or discomfort.
Supervised Exercise Therapy.Coverage: Covered benefit by Medicare and most commercial insurances.

Structured Community-Based Exercise Prog.Settings: Program occurs in the personal setting of the patient.
Structured Community-Based Exercise Prog.Supervision: Qualified health care professionals prescribe an exercise regimen, and the program is self-directed.
Structured Community-Based Exercise Prog.Content: Patient counseling ensures understanding of how to begin and maintain the program.
Structured Community-Based Exercise Prog.Incorporation: May incorporate behavioral change techniques and include periodic supervised sessions.

General Findings.Mechanisms: Improvements in skeletal muscle strength and endurance, endothelial function, potential angiogenesis, and blood distribution efficiency.
General Findings.Local Inflammatory Response: No increase in microvascular or macrovascular blood flow after exercise training despite significant functional improvement.
General Findings.Evidence for Community-Based Programs: Recent studies support community-based exercise programs in this patient population.

Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.1: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD, SET is recommended to improve walking performance, functional status, and QOL.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.2: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD, a structured community-based exercise program with behavioral change techniques is effective in improving walking performance, functional status, and QOL.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.3: In patients who have undergone revascularization for chronic symptomatic PAD, SET after revascularization is effective to improve walking performance, functional status, and QOL.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.4: In patients with functionally limiting claudication, SET or a structured community-based exercise program should be offered as an initial treatment option.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.5: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD, alternative programs of nonwalking structured exercise therapy can be beneficial to improve walking performance, functional status, and QOL.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.6: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD, the usefulness of structured walking exercise that avoids moderate to severe ischemic symptoms is uncertain.
Recommendations for Exercise Therapy for.7: In patients with chronic symptomatic PAD, the usefulness of unstructured exercise to improve walking performance, functional status, and QOL is uncertain.

content.Synopsis: Strong, high-quality, and consistent data support the use of SET to improve functional status, walking performance, and QOL in patients with chronic symptomatic PAD. This includes those who are being considered for revascularization as exercise improvements are at least as effective as revascularization. Medicare and most commercial insurance companies now cover SET for patients with claudication. Some third-party payers may require SET to be performed in a hospital outpatient setting. When revascularization is performed, referral to SET can result in optimization of benefits.

content.1: The document discusses the durability and efficacy of Supervised Exercise Therapy (SET) for patients with symptomatic Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) and diabetes. SET shows a favorable risk-benefit ratio, although it is challenging for some patients, especially those with exercise limitations or amputations.
content.2: Community-based exercise programs are highlighted as effective for those with chronic symptomatic PAD. Studies support these programs for improving functional status and managing claudication symptoms, showing improvements over non-exercising control groups.
content.3: The document also mentions trials comparing SET to other treatments like Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty (PTA) and combined therapy for PAD, indicating better outcomes in certain combinations.
content.4: Studies such as the CLEVER trial compared exercise with endovascular revascularization, showing both methods were superior to optimal medical care.

content.summary: The guideline discusses the outcomes of endovascular revascularization and supervised exercise therapy (SET) in treating peripheral artery disease (PAD) in patients. It indicates that SET is effective for patients with stable claudication, improving functional status and quality of life (QOL). Studies support the use of structured exercise programs for chronic symptomatic PAD. However, the utility of unstructured exercise is uncertain. For asymptomatic patients, revascularization should not be used solely for preventing disease progression.
key_points: SET is recommended for patients with limiting claudication and no evidence of critical limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI).
Alternative exercise regimens offer health benefits and include non-walking exercises.
Revascularization should be carefully considered in asymptomatic PAD patients and not performed solely for disease progression prevention.
recommendations: In patients with asymptomatic PAD, consider revascularization to support other procedures but not solely for disease prevention.

content.Synopsis: Patients with PAD may be asymptomatic despite having significant arterial lesions. The focus is on patients with known arterial lesions who have no symptoms. Revascularization procedures are rarely indicated for asymptomatic patients due to the increased risk of major adverse limb events (MALE) after revascularization.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Endovascular or surgical revascularization can reconstruct diseased arteries to facilitate necessary procedures. Techniques include conduit placement, stenting, and other ancillaries for safe sheath insertion/removal.
Some asymptomatic PAD patients progress to symptomatic disease; however, early revascularization for asymptomatic patients is not recommended due to risks of complications, especially MALE.
content.Revascularization Techniques: Revascularization for PAD and CLTI is extensively studied, mainly for claudication or severe PAD presentations. Recommendations are provided for endovascular/surgical revascularization, but device-specific technical aspects are not addressed.
content.Data Registries: Data registries like VQI, NCDR, PVI, and VIRTEX track revascularization approaches and patient outcomes to improve local quality initiatives.
content.Revascularization for Claudication: Chronic symptomatic PAD patients have leg symptoms but no rest pain or tissue loss. Treatments include medical therapy and exercise. Revascularization is for patients unresponsive to these therapies.

content.Title: Algorithm for Revascularization for Claudication (Chronic Symptomatic PAD)
content.Focus: Guideline for revascularization in patients with claudication due to chronic symptomatic Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD).
Key Steps: Assess if symptoms are functionally limiting and response to GDMT (including structured exercise) is inadequate.
Conduct risk-benefit assessment to determine if revascularization benefits outweigh risks.
Consider revascularization if benefits outweigh risks. Options include:

Key Steps.Condition: Hemodynamically significant aortoiliac or femoropopliteal disease
Options: Surgical revascularization is reasonable if perioperative risk is acceptable.

Key Steps.Condition: Hemodynamically significant common femoral artery disease
Key Steps.Option: Endarterectomy is reasonable, particularly to preserve profunda femoris artery pathways.

Key Steps.Condition: High risk for surgery or favorable anatomic factors

Key Steps.Note: Effectiveness of endovascular or surgical revascularization is unknown.

Revascularization for Claudication: Init.1: In patients with functionally limiting claudication who are being considered for revascularization, potential benefits with respect to QOL, walking performance, and overall functional status should be weighed against the risks and durability of intervention and possible need for repeated procedures.
Revascularization for Claudication: Init.2a: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and an inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), revascularization is a reasonable treatment option to improve walking function and QOL.
Revascularization for Claudication: Init.3: In patients with claudication who have had an adequate clinical response to GDMT (including structured exercise), revascularization is not recommended.

Revascularization for Claudication: Aort.1: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant aortoiliac or femoropopliteal disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), endovascular revascularization is effective to improve walking performance and QOL.
Revascularization for Claudication: Aort.2a: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant aortoiliac or femoropopliteal disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), surgical revascularization is reasonable if perioperative risk is acceptable and technical factors suggest advantages over endovascular approaches.

Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.2a: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant common femoral artery disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), surgical endarterectomy is reasonable, especially if endovascular approaches adversely affect profunda femoris artery pathways.
Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.2b: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant common femoral artery disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), endovascular approaches may be considered in those at high risk for surgical revascularization and/or if anatomical factors are favorable.

Revascularization for Claudication: Infr.2b: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and isolated hemodynamically significant infrapopliteal disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), the effectiveness of endovascular revascularization is unknown.
Revascularization for Claudication: Infr.2b_2: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and isolated hemodynamically significant infrapopliteal disease with inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), the effectiveness of surgical revascularization is unknown.

content.Title: 2024 Peripheral Artery Disease Guideline
Topics Covered: Treatment planning considering patient's symptoms and history
Key Points: Revascularization is based on anatomical evaluation, patient symptoms, and potential benefits and risks.
Patient discussions are critical before revascularization to understand personalized treatment goals.
Endovascular options include PTA and other catheter-based techniques.
Surgical revascularization typically involves the removal of plaque or bypassing occluded segments.
Patient factors like diabetes, renal disease, and obesity can affect revascularization outcomes.
Revascularization improves QOL, walking distance, and pain-free walking, especially in multilevel PAD cases.
Ongoing patient evaluation is important in managing PAD.

content.Patients with Claudication: Patients who have had an adequate response to medical therapies, including structured exercise, and no longer have significant impairment in functional status and quality of life do not require revascularization. Revascularization may improve symptoms but may also lead to restenosis and other risks.
content.Revascularization for Claudication: RCTs have shown that revascularization improves walking performance and QOL in patients with aortoiliac and/or femoropopliteal disease. The combination of endovascular therapy plus SET is often more effective than other treatment modalities.
content.Long-term Patency and Complications: Long-term vessel patency is greater in the aortoiliac than the femoropopliteal segment. Factors affecting the patency include lesion length, occlusion, and other patient factors like smoking, diabetes, etc.
content.Clinical Trials: Various RCTs have shown the efficacy of endovascular treatment for claudication, with differences in benefits depending on whether the treatment was for aortoiliac or femoropopliteal disease.
content.Individualized Approach: The decision for revascularization should be made based on the patient's goals and factors such as lesion length and surgical risk.

Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.Trials: No randomized trials for common femoral endarterectomy versus medical therapy.
Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.Outcomes: Low risk of 30-day mortality and low early reintervention rates.
Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.Complications: Wound infection (3.4%), lymphatic fistula (3.4%), and need for procedure-related local revision (8.6%).
Revascularization for Claudication: Comm.Patency Rates: Excellent patency rates and similar 1-year patency when compared with common femoral endovascular interventions.

Revascularization for Claudication: Infr.Usage: Isolated infrapopliteal disease is rare and typically occurs in patients with CLTI.
Revascularization for Claudication: Infr.Procedure Efficacy: No RCTs comparing surgical revascularization versus medical therapy.
Revascularization for Claudication: Infr.Registry Findings: Infrapopliteal bypasses with autogenous vein were associated with higher complication rates.

Recommendation for Surgical Revasculariz.For Functionally Limiting Claudication: Bypass to the popliteal artery with autogenous vein is preferred over prosthetic graft material.

content.Synopsis: The document discusses the management of femoral-popliteal bypass surgery as a common procedure for patients with Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) suffering from stenosis or occlusion. The choice of conduit, autogenous vein versus prosthetic, is important for surgical success. The guideline emphasizes the need for team-based care in managing Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI), focusing on wound healing, minimizing tissue loss, and preserving ambulatory status.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with CLTI, a multispecialty care team should evaluate and provide comprehensive care, including wound healing, tissue preservation, and ambulatory status.

content.Management of CLTI: Patients with CLTI have advanced PAD. Team-based care is recommended, with revascularization as a standard treatment to minimize tissue loss and maintain a functional limb. Care also involves infection management and pressure offloading.

content.Title: Algorithm for Management of CLTI
content.Guideline Summary: The document outlines an algorithm for managing chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It emphasizes evaluation and care by a multidisciplinary team, wound care, and management of foot infections. It discusses revascularization options, 'no option' therapies, and pressure offloading for ulcers.
Patient Pathway: Patients with CLTI receive guideline-directed management therapy (GDMT).
Evaluation and care by a multispecialty care team, focusing on wound care and management of foot infections.
Consideration of surgical, endovascular, or hybrid revascularization based on patient anatomy and preferences.
For non-candidates, focus on wound care and 'no option' therapies such as amputation.
Pressure offloading ulcers for both diabetic and non-diabetic cases.
content.Multispecialty Care: Team should include expertise in medical management of diabetes, wound care, infection management, and rehabilitation.
content.Objective: The strategy is aimed at preserving a functional limb while evaluating all revascularization and therapeutic options.

content.Multispecialty Care Team for PAD: A team of professionals from different specialties assists in the evaluation and management of patients with PAD, especially those with CLTI. This includes vascular medical and surgical specialists, nurses, podiatrists, wound care specialists, endocrinologists, internal medicine specialists, infectious disease specialists, diagnostic radiologists, pharmacists, physical medicine and rehabilitation clinicians, social workers, and more.
content.Revascularization for CLTI: Recommendations for revascularization in CLTI include surgical, endovascular, or hybrid techniques to minimize tissue loss and preserve limb function. Evaluation for revascularization should be conducted by a multispecialty team before amputation. Considerations in revascularization strategy involve patient preferences, anatomy, and available conduits. Ultrasound mapping is recommended for surgical candidates.

content.Synopsis: The central component of care for patients with CLTI (chronic limb-threatening ischemia) is revascularization to improve blood flow, prevent amputation, minimize tissue loss, heal wounds, and relieve pain. Surgical options include bypass and endarterectomy, and endovascular techniques are available. The BEST-CLI and BASIL-2 trials provide guidance for revascularization strategies. Factors influencing strategies are detailed in Table 16.
content.Recommendation: Patients with CLTI should be assessed for revascularization due to the high risk of amputation. Endovascular, surgical, and hybrid approaches have been shown to prevent amputation. Techniques also apply to PAD, with factors such as failed prior procedures, lesion characteristics, and anatomy affecting modality choice.

content.Clinical Focus: Revascularization strategies for Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI).
Key Findings: Hybrid procedures have similar patency and limb salvage rates compared to open surgical reconstruction.
Single-segment saphenous vein bypass leads to fewer major adverse limb events (MALE) compared to alternative conduits.
Endovascular vs surgical revascularization differences in outcomes are context-dependent.
Important Trials: BEST-CLI trial: compared surgical bypass and endovascular revascularization.
BASIL-2 trial: investigated bypass vs endovascular in patients with infrainguinal disease.
Guidelines and Recommendations: Evaluation of different revascularization strategies based on the individual patient condition and anatomy.
Recommendation for the use of autogenous vein bypass for infrapopliteal arteries.
General Observations: Bypass using autogenous vein or composite bypasses is effective for revascularization.
Strategic decision-making process emphasized for revascularization.

general_observations: Autogenous veins, especially the great saphenous vein, are preferred for femoral-popliteal bypass.
Vein mapping is performed using duplex ultrasound to assess vein patency and other features.
Prosthetic conduits are secondary options when autogenous veins are unsuitable.
Revascularization for CLTI focuses on maximizing perfusion to the wound bed.
Direct revascularization demonstrates superior wound healing compared to indirect methods.

key_points.point: Studies have demonstrated improved patency of femoral-popliteal bypass when constructed with autogenous vein.
key_points.reference: References to BEST-CLI and BASIL-2 protocols for establishing suitability of conduits.

key_points.point: Saphenous vein evaluation includes assessment of vein patency and length, alongside possible presence of thrombosis.
key_points.reference: Ultrasound vein mapping and consideration of previous interventions.

key_points.point: Cadaveric vein grafts are considered for patients lacking suitable autogenous veins.
key_points.reference: Patency studies and clinical outcomes of surgical revascularization methods.

key_points.point: Revascularization strategies aim to provide direct pulsatile flow to the wound bed through angiosomes.
Multi-level disease management includes addressing outflow lesions post initial intervention if ischemic pain persists.
key_points.reference: Observation on optimization of inflow and outflow to improve wound healing.

recommendations.description: Patients with CLTI and diabetic foot ulcers should receive pressure offloading, when possible, to promote tissue growth and wound healing.
Patients with PAD and previous diabetic foot ulcers should be referred for customized footwear.
Patients with CLTI and foot ulcers without diabetes may be considered for pressure offloading.

content.synopsis: Pressure offloading is crucial for managing patients with CLTI, especially those with diabetic foot ulcers. Customized footwear can play a key role in reducing recurrence and promoting healing, especially when properly adhered to.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with CLTI, prompt management of foot infection with antibiotics, debridement, and other surgical management is recommended.
In patients with CLTI with nonhealing wounds, wound care should be provided to optimize the wound-healing environment after revascularization with the goal of complete wound healing.
In patients with CLTI with nonhealing diabetic foot ulcers, hyperbaric oxygen therapy may be considered to assist in wound healing after revascularization.

content.Synopsis: Management of infection and local wound care are critical for patients with CLTI and must be implemented with revascularization. The care team should include wound care plan development and implementation.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Foot Infection: Prompt diagnosis and initiation of therapy with antibiotics and management reduce risk of amputation and death. Surgical debridement is recommended for foot infections.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Wound Care for Patients with Diabetes: Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) can be used post-revascularization. Wounds require maintaining a moist wound bed and controlling exudation.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Specifics: Wound care involves surgical, mechanical, enzymatic, or chemical debridement. Nonhealing wounds in diabetics are complex and multifactorial.

Components of Wound Care for Patients Wi: Management of infection, inflammation, or both
Medical optimization of host factors (e.g., smoking cessation, glycemic control)

Studies Discussed.Population: Patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI), diabetic foot ulcers
Findings: Mixed results with hyperbaric oxygen therapy
Recent systematic review showing potential for wound healing

Studies Discussed.RCT: Focused on foot ulcers and PAD
Findings: Significant decrease in ulcer area at 6 weeks without revascularization
No difference in ulcer size at 6 months

Recommendations for 'No Option' Patients.Recommendation: Consider venous arterialization for limb preservation

content.CLTI: CLTI is a severe manifestation of PAD. A classification system for patients with no revascularization options is based on anatomic categories. Treatments include venous arterialization, intermittent pneumatic compression devices, and prostanoids. Gene therapy and growth factors are not available in practice. Lumbar sympathectomy is a palliative procedure rarely used.
content.Prostanoids: Considered for 'no-option' CLTI as they have vasodilatory effects, inhibit platelet aggregation, and protect vascular endothelium. Meta-analysis showed a lower risk of major amputations, but no total amputation reduction. Moderate-quality evidence showed increased adverse events.
content.Intermittent Pneumatic Compression: Alternative treatment for 'no-option' CLTI, increases leg blood flow and may improve outcomes. Systematic reviews suggest decreased amputation rates and improved quality of life, though data is limited and shows some risk of bias.

content.Title: Recommendations for Amputation for CLTI
General Information: CLTI (Chronic Limb Threatening Ischemia) is associated with poor health-related quality of life regardless of treatment approach.
Preservation of a functional limb with a shoeable foot is a primary goal in patients with CLTI.
The use of prosthetics and resumption of walking are important outcomes for quality of life in amputees.
Venous arterialization is a novel approach for 'no-option' patients, connecting a proximal artery to a distal vein to allow blood to enter the foot through the venous system.
Systematic reviews and studies report varied rates of limb salvage and wound healing dependent on the treatment method used.

Recommendations.Text: In patients with CLTI who require amputation, evaluation should be performed by a multispecialty care team to assess for the most distal level of amputation that facilitates healing and provides maximal functional ability.
In patients with CLTI, primary amputation is indicated when life over limb is the prevailing consideration and clinical factors suggest the threatened limb is the cause of patient's instability.
In patients with CLTI, a patient-centered approach using objective classification, patient risk, and anatomic pattern of disease combined with patient and family goals is recommended.
In patients undergoing minor amputation, a customized program of follow-up care including wound care, pressure offloading, and use of therapeutic footwear is recommended.
Retrospective assessment of institutional outcomes (including amputation) with objective limb threat classification tools can be useful for quality improvement.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Multispecialty care team collaboration i: The text outlines the importance of foot surveillance to reduce wound recurrence. In patients with advanced soft-tissue infection, emergency amputation may be necessary to avoid patient death. It also discusses the use of classification tools to facilitate assessment and documentation.

topics: Guidance for patients with extensive tissue destruction and surgical resection considerations.
Management for patients with CLTI who have undergone minor amputation.
Use of tools like WiFI and GLASS for outcome validation in CLTI patient care.
Registry and data collection efforts such as the VQI and NCDR.
Acute Limb Ischemia (ALI) definitions and treatment categories.

sections.title: Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI) Management
details: Primary amputation considered after thorough specialist review.
Longitudinal care for post-amputation patients with CLTI.
Tools like WiFI and GLASS for prognosis and outcomes.

sections.title: Data and Registry Information
details: The Vascular Quality Initiative (VQI) registry collects clinical, procedural, and outcome data.
NCDR and other registries assist in benchmarking procedural outcomes.

sections.title: Acute Limb Ischemia (ALI)
details: ALI defined by symptom duration <2 weeks.
Categories I, II, and III limb states based on salvageability.

content.title: Initial Clinical Evaluation and Diagnostic Approach to ALI

recommendations.text: Patients with ALI should be evaluated on an emergency basis by a clinician with sufficient experience to assess limb viability and implement appropriate therapy.

In patients with suspected ALI, the initial clinical evaluation should rapidly assess limb viability and potential for salvage and can be achieved without noninvasive imaging (ie, duplex ultrasound, CTA, or MRA).

In patients with ALI who have a complicated history of revascularization procedures, it may be reasonable to obtain noninvasive imaging (ie, duplex ultrasound, CTA, or MRA) before deciding to proceed with revascularization.

content.algorithm: Algorithm for Diagnosis and Management of ALI
flowchart: Initial assessment of acutely cold, painful, pulseless leg (symptoms <14d).
Emergency clinical evaluation including symptoms, motor, and sensory assessment, arterial and venous Doppler signals.
Categories based on viability: Category I (Viable), Category II (Threatened), Category III (Irreversible).
Revascularization and anticoagulation recommended based on category.
Other management options: monitor for compartment syndrome, prophylactic fasciotomy.
additional_information: Unfractionated heparin is recommended unless contraindicated.
Comprehensive history, physical examination, and tests for acute cardiovascular causes are part of the management.

content.Synopsis: ALI (Acute Limb Ischemia) is a vascular emergency requiring rapid recognition, assessment of limb viability, and timely treatment to maximize limb salvage. Initial evaluation includes targeted history and physical examination and the use of Doppler devices to assess arterial and venous signals.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Patients with ALI should be evaluated on an emergency basis by experienced clinicians. If local expertise is unavailable, patient transfer should be considered.
ALI must be recognized rapidly; skeletal muscle will tolerate ischemia for 4 to 6 hours. Rapid assessment of blood flow and limb viability is critical.
Lower-extremity symptoms of ALI include pain, loss of sensation, and loss of function. Prolonged symptoms reduce limb salvage possibility.
Bedside clinical assessment includes evaluating pain intensity, motor and sensory defects, and using continuous wave Doppler to distinguish threatened from nonviable limbs.
Comorbidities should be identified, but not delay therapy. Noninvasive imaging studies are generally unnecessary unless revascularization strategy requires it.
Patients undergoing lower extremity revascularization present unique challenges, requiring an expedited decision-making strategy.
Recommendation for Revascularization: In patients with ALI and a salvageable limb, revascularization (endovascular or surgical) is recommended to prevent amputation.
In patients with ALI and a salvageable limb treated with catheter-directed thrombolysis, adjunctive revascularization can be useful.
In individuals with ALI from chemotherapeutic or prothrombotic states, a deliberate planning strategy is recommended before definitive revascularization.
In patients with ALI and a nonsalvageable limb, revascularization should not be performed.

content.Synopsis: Revascularization is indicated in patients with acute limb ischemia (ALI) and a salvageable limb to prevent irreversible tissue damage and major amputation. Techniques such as surgical thromboembolectomy and catheter-based thrombolysis are effective. Ultrasound-accelerated, catheter-based thrombolysis, and mechanical trombectomy are newer developments. Safety in using these techniques is backed by studies when treating ALI. Patient-specific and local factors are crucial in selecting the strategy. Monitoring for compartment syndrome and reperfusion injury is necessary.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Recommendation: Revascularization should be implemented in patients with ALI and a salvageable limb.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Rationale: There is an association between ALI and high rates of mortality and limb loss. Rapid intervention, like catheter-based thrombolysis as a revascularization strategy, has been investigated for its effectiveness compared to surgery. However, literature shows variable definitions of ALI, and studies have shown mixed outcomes related to safety and efficacy.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Additional Considerations: Further evaluation is warranted in patients with ALI associated with systemic proinflammatory and prothrombotic states. ALI as a complication is also observed with dense illness presentations such as COVID-19 and severe cases with multiorgan failure.

content.title: Recommendations for Adjunctive Therapies to Minimize Tissue Loss in ALI
content.synopsis: Patients presenting with Acute Limb Ischemia (ALI) experience high rates of morbidity, mortality, and limb loss. Timely revascularization is the goal, but poor outcomes may still occur. Adjuncts to revascularization, like fasciotomy, help minimize tissue loss. Compartment syndrome is a significant concern, often developing due to reperfusion injury.

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with ALI should be monitored and treated for compartment syndrome with fasciotomy after revascularization to prevent reperfusion injury.
In patients with ALI and a threatened but salvageable limb, prophylactic fasciotomy is reasonable.
In patients with ALI and prolonged ischemia, early amputation can be beneficial to avoid morbidity of reperfusion.

key_points: Elevated compartment pressures are not common initially in ALI but can occur with reperfusion injury.
Oxygen-free radicals create leaky capillaries, raising compartment pressures.
High clinical suspicion is necessary to diagnose compartment syndrome.

content.Guideline Overview: Patients with ALI should be evaluated for revascularization and receive medical therapy concurrently. Systemic anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin is recommended on diagnosis unless contraindicated.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Systemic anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin should be administered to all patients with ALI at diagnosis, regardless of cause or anatomic level of occlusion.

content.Considerations: In patients at high risk of bleeding, or with contraindications such as aortic dissection or major vascular trauma, alternative therapies or management of anticoagulation may be necessary. Delayed primary closure of fasciotomy incisions can mitigate wound morbidity.

content.title: Recommendations for Diagnostic Evaluation for the Cause of ALI

recommendations.text: In patients with ALI, a comprehensive medical history and physical examination should be performed to determine the cause of thrombosis or embolization.
In patients with ALI, testing for a cardiovascular cause of thromboembolism can be useful.

content.synopsis: ALI is a vascular emergency that requires rapid diagnosis and management, including timely revascularization to reperfuse the ischemic limb. Causes of ALI include acute thrombosis of native vessel or bypass grafts in the setting of known diagnosis of PAD, arterial embolism, dissection, and trauma.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Underlying PAD is a common cause of ALI. Among patients with PAD, the rate of ALI is 0.8% to 1.7%.
Most patients with ALI attributable to underlying PAD have a history of lower extremity revascularization, amputation, or lower baseline ABI values.
A targeted history regarding known PAD, including previous revascularization procedures, should be obtained.
Beyond PAD, other key causes of ALI include native vessel thrombosis and systemic embolization.

content.General Information: The guideline focuses on the evaluation and management of Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) with emphasis on rapid targeted evaluation of ischemic limbs, assessment of potential cardiac causes, and long-term follow-up. It highlights the importance of assessing limb symptoms, functional status, and regular monitoring to prevent adverse events.

General Principles: Routine clinical evaluation for PAD patients, including assessment of limb symptoms, functional status, and risk factor management.
Coordination of care among clinicians to manage PAD and comorbid conditions.
Functional Status and QOL: Periodic assessment of functional status and overall health-related quality of life for PAD patients.
Medical Therapy: Long-term use of GDMT to prevent MACE and MALE.
Postrevascularization Follow-Up: For patients with lower extremity revascularization, periodic clinical evaluation of symptoms and foot assessment is recommended.

content.Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD): PAD is a chronic medical condition requiring comprehensive care and ongoing surveillance, especially after revascularization. Emphasis is placed on cardiovascular and limb risk reduction through medical therapy and structured exercise.

Lower Extremity Revascularization: For patients with PAD who have undergone lower extremity revascularization and show new signs or symptoms, ABI and arterial duplex ultrasound is recommended.
Surveillance should occur within 1 to 3 months postprocedure, and then periodically (e.g., 6 to 12 months).
Endovascular and Infraininguinal Procedu: Routine surveillance with ABI and arterial duplex ultrasound is reasonable for those without new symptoms.
Recommendations.Telehealth: Can be used for vascular evaluation and management in PAD patients, with follow-ups tailored to urgency.

Follow-up Care.Individualized: Follow-up care and visits should be based on severity, history, and timing of previous procedures.
Follow-up Care.Risk Factor Management: Coordination of care is critical, with risk factors including diabetes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia.

content.Outcome Improvement: Effective risk factor management can significantly improve MACE-free years in patients.

content.General Information: The clinical guideline discusses the assessment of functional status and quality of life (QOL) in patients with peripheral artery disease (PAD). It emphasizes the importance of patient-reported health status measures for PAD, highlighting tools like VascuQoL-6, PADQOL, and PAQ. Depression screening and management are also mentioned as crucial due to its association with adverse outcomes. Functional capacity can be assessed informally or through objective tests like the 6-minute walk test (6MWT). Lipid-lowering therapies reduce MACEs and MALE events in PAD patients. Antiplatelet and antithrombotic therapies are vital for preventing MACEs. The management of diabetes and hypertension is stressed, along with smoking cessation.
content.Medical Therapy and Preventive Foot Care: Patient-specific GDMT is important for preventing MACE and MALE events in PAD patients. Smoking cessation is critical for PAD patients. Both endovascular and surgical revascularization complications including restenosis are discussed. The use of duplex ultrasound for surveillance after revascularization is emphasized.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses the surveillance and management of peripheral artery disease (PAD), focusing on revascularization procedures. It highlights the limitations and benefits of various methods such as duplex ultrasound and telehealth, identifies gaps in evidence, and outlines priorities for future research and advocacy in the field of PAD.
Clinical Recommendations: Duplex ultrasound is reliable for infrainguinal autogenous vein bypass grafts.
Surveillance after endovascular revascularization by anatomic level is explored.
Telehealth can be used for patients with stable, symptomatic PAD but has limitations.
Development of effective medical therapies and improved functional status metrics in PAD patients is prioritized.
RCTs and registry data are needed to analyze outcomes for specific patient groups, such as those with chronic symptomatic PAD.
Evidence Gaps: Screening for asymptomatic PAD and implementation of therapies for cardiovascular risk reduction.
Trials on medical therapies to prevent MACE (Major Adverse Cardiac Events) and MALE (Major Adverse Limb Events).
Determining optimal antiplatelet and antithrombotic regimens post-revascularization.
Shared decision-making strategies for managing chronic symptomatic PAD and CLTI (Critical Limb Threatening Ischemia).
Advocacy Priorities: Collaborative teamwork to expand access and improve patient outcomes.

content: Implementation of the PAD National Action Plan with goals to improve awareness, detection, and treatment of PAD nationwide.
Dissemination and implementation of guidelines to improve detection and care for patients with PAD (including CLTI) aiming at reducing nontraumatic amputations by 20% by 2030.
Focus on quality outcomes in PAD including prescription of GDMT, smoking cessation, and prevention of readmissions after revascularization.
Development of AHA Get With The Guidelines initiatives to address disparities and risk factor modification between PAD and CAD.
Improved access to, affordability of, and utilization of SET for PAD, including development of hybrid programs incorporating telehealth.
Interventions to address racial disparity gaps in amputation and revascularization, focusing on improving limb and cardiovascular outcomes.
Use of telehealth and remote devices to provide PAD-related care, especially for underserved areas and patient populations needing more intensive follow-up, such as CLTI.
Creation of a national registry of nontraumatic lower extremity amputation for identifying improvement opportunities and unmasking treatment disparities.

content.Title: 2024 Peripheral Artery Disease Guideline

It includes references to various clinical guidelines and procedural standards provided by different committees and associations, such as the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and the American Heart Association (AHA).
The guidelines cover the evaluation, management, and treatment strategies of PAD in various contexts, including preoperative evaluation and noncardiac surgery.
There is emphasis on guidelines for patients with coronary artery disease, diabetes, and cholesterol management.
Updates on recent ACC/AHA guidelines are incorporated.
Contemporary issues like the role of tobacco cessation in treatment and advanced technologies in cardiovascular interventions are also discussed.

Elderly patients with PAD have an increased risk of complications such as amputations.
Controlling tobacco use, antiplatelets, and lipid-lowering therapies are essential in management.

context: In patients with PAD who are on maximally tolerated statin therapy and have an LDL-C level of ≥70 mg/dL, it is reasonable to add PCSK9 inhibitor therapy. In a subgroup analysis of studies evaluating PCSK9 inhibitors, use of alirocumab and evolocumab in patients with PAD were associated with lower rates of MACE and MALE compared with placebo.

This recommendation for the use of PCSK9 inhibitors in patients with PAD who have not achieved an LDL target of <70 mg/dL aligns with the 2018 AHA/ACC cholesterol guideline recommendation for patients with clinical atherosclerotic CVD.
